skill.name: specs-creator
skill.description: Creates structured PRD, technical specification, and UX specification documents from feature requirements using bundled templates and validation tasks.
skill.summary: Guides structured PRD, technical specification, and UX specification creation.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 241 issues and a 100/100 risk score, but review found the high and critical alerts are mostly keyword false positives in templates and validators. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential theft, destructive behavior, or confirmed malicious intent was found. The real risk is that the skill writes local spec files and asks agents to run bundled Python validation scripts, so it should publish with a filesystem and script execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Bundled Validation Scripts Process Local Filesystem Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill workflow asks agents to run local Python validation scripts. The scripts read Markdown files, detect specs directories, and recursively inspect spec files with depth and result limits. This supports legitimate documentation validation but should be disclosed because it operates on local project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of local file reads and recursive Markdown discovery. Confidence is below very high because the scripts include size, extension, symlink, depth, and result limits and no malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Skill Writes Specification Files in the Workspace
medium_findings.description: The task files direct the agent to save generated PRD, technical specification, and UX specification documents under specs/. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it modifies the user workspace and relies on the overwrite safeguards in the instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write destinations and overwrite restrictions are explicit in the task and skill instructions. This is a real workspace modification risk, but it is bounded to documentation output.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Keyword Alerts Are Template Context
low_findings.description: Critical and high alerts for ransomware, C2, API keys, and database URLs occur in template examples for disaster recovery, deployment, and environment variable documentation. These lines are not executable and do not exfiltrate secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is clearly inside documentation templates and example sections. It names incident response and deployment concepts rather than implementing malware behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Sensitive File Alerts Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: Many high and critical findings point to words such as design, system, md, and template labels. No weak cryptographic API, Windows SAM access, certificate file read, or secret file handling was found at these reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show enum names, file-extension patterns, section names, and UX template wording. There is no cryptographic operation or sensitive database access at those locations.

low_findings.title: Backtick Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The static external command findings in Markdown files are mostly inline paths, fenced examples, and task wording. They do not execute by themselves, although the workflow does separately instruct agents to run validation scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown syntax and example command text, not runtime shell execution. Confidence is high, with the caveat that the skill also has an intended script-running workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Python Script Execution Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs agents to run bundled validation scripts. No dangerous command construction was found, but script execution is a meaningful marketplace risk factor.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow and script entry points are explicit. The risk is operational rather than malicious because reviewed code performs validation and exits with status codes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Markdown Discovery and Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The dependency validator recursively discovers Markdown files under a specs directory while skipping symlinks and limiting depth and result count. This is controlled filesystem access, not exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly traverses local directories with explicit safeguards. It is a real data-access behavior but remains scoped to validation.

content.user_title: Create Product, Tech, and UX Specs
content.value_statement: Feature work can stall when product, engineering, and design requirements are scattered. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PRD, technical, and UX specifications.
actual_capabilities: Creates PRD, technical specification, and UX specification documents from feature requirements.
Uses separate templates for product, engineering, and UX documentation.
Enforces the dependency order from app vision to PRD, technical specs, and UX specs.
Directs generated documents to the project specs directory.
Includes validation scripts for document structure and dependency checks.
limitations: It depends on existing prerequisite documents before later specs can be created.
It validates structure and dependencies, not business accuracy or product strategy.
It requires user approval before overwriting existing specification files.
Template examples and placeholders still need project-specific review.

use_cases.title: Turn Vision Into a PRD
use_cases.description: Convert an app vision document into a product requirements document with goals, user stories, requirements, and success metrics.

use_cases.title: Prepare Engineering Specifications
use_cases.description: Transform approved product requirements into technical architecture, data model, API, security, testing, and deployment sections.

use_cases.title: Document UX Decisions
use_cases.description: Create a UX specification that covers flows, components, responsive behavior, accessibility, motion, and design checklist items.

prompt_templates.title: Create a PRD
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PRD for this feature using specs/app-vision.md. Ask me for any missing product details before writing the file.

prompt_templates.title: Create Technical Specs
prompt_templates.scenario: Engineering planning after a PRD exists.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create technical specs from specs/prd.md. Include architecture, data models, API details, security, testing, and deployment notes.

prompt_templates.title: Create UX Specs
prompt_templates.scenario: Design planning after product and technical specs exist.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create UX specs using specs/prd.md and specs/tech-specs.md. Cover flows, components, accessibility, responsive behavior, and design states.

prompt_templates.title: Review the Spec Chain
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the app vision, PRD, technical specs, and UX specs for missing dependencies, contradictions, and unclear decisions. Suggest focused revisions.

output_examples.input: Create PRD for a collaborative notes app from the app vision.
output: A PRD saved in the specs directory.
Sections for overview, goals, user stories, requirements, scope, and success metrics.
A short completion report with validation status and next steps.

output_examples.input: Create technical specs after the PRD is approved.
output: A technical specification with architecture, components, data models, APIs, security, testing, and deployment sections.
Dependency checks that confirm prerequisite documents exist.
A summary of gaps that need user clarification.

output_examples.input: Create UX specs for the approved product and technical plan.
output: A UX specification covering design principles, flows, components, responsive layouts, accessibility, motion, and checklist items.
Notes on any incomplete product or engineering information.
A final report with file location and recommended review steps.

best_practices: Start with a clear app vision before requesting the PRD.
Review each generated spec before creating the next dependent document.
Run validation after edits so structure and dependency issues are visible.
anti_patterns: Do not skip prerequisite documents to create later specs faster.
Do not treat generated specs as final without stakeholder review.
Do not leave bracketed placeholders or example comments in approved specs.

faq.question: What documents can this skill create?
faq.answer: It can create PRDs, technical specifications, and UX specifications using bundled templates.

faq.question: Does it write application code?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructions explicitly limit work to documentation and specification files.

faq.question: What prerequisite files are required?
faq.answer: A PRD requires an app vision. Technical specs require a PRD. UX specs require both PRD and technical specs.

faq.question: Where are generated files saved?
faq.answer: The task files direct outputs to the project specs directory, such as specs/prd.md and specs/tech-specs.md.

faq.question: Does the skill include validation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python scripts that check structure and document dependencies for Markdown specification files.

faq.question: Can it replace product or security review?
faq.answer: No. It improves document structure, but humans must review decisions, requirements, risks, and security assumptions.
